segments.s241-g73: 'همت گویم' places the noun همت ('resolve, purpose') before the verb گویم ('I say/I will say'), a common Persian order where the theme is fronted for emphasis; گویم is first-person present (classical/poetic) 'I say' or 'I will say'. The conjunction و links the two verbs, and هم before پذیرم marks 'also/too'; پذیرم is first-person present 'I accept' and takes سپاس (thanks/praise) as its object following the verb.
segments.s241-g74: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' introducing the next imperative clause. بشنو‌ای is the imperative 'listen' with the vocative particle -ای attached (poetic/archaic vocative). جفت here is a vocative noun 'pair' (addressing two persons or a coupled addressee) and نیکی‌شناس is a compound meaning 'one who knows/recognizes goodness' or 'appreciative person', modifying/identifying that addressee.
segments.s241-g57: مرا ... بشنو is an imperative clause: مرا (object 'me') precedes the imperative بشنو ('hear' / 'listen'), so the literal order is 'hear me'. مهربان یار is a vocative placed between object and verb ('my kind beloved' / 'kind friend'). چه گفت is a subordinate interrogative clause 'what he said' (چه = 'what'; گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said').
segments.s241-g58: ازان پس که introduces a temporal subordinate: 'after that, when...'. که here introduces the clause. با کام is an adverbial phrase 'with (our) desire/fulfillment' and گشتیم is the past 1st‑pl. of گشتن used causatively/ intransitively 'we became/turned', with the predicate noun جفت ('paired') following the verb: 'we became a pair.'
segments.s241-g59: بپیمای here is an exhortative/request form ('stay/hold on' or 'be patient') addressed to the addressee; می‌تا is a temporal subordinating particle 'until/as long as'; یکی داستان is the direct object 'one tale' (یکی = 'one/a certain', داستان = 'story').
segments.s241-g60: بگویمت is a 1st‑person verb with enclitic object 't' meaning 'I will tell you' or 'let me tell you' (future/intentional). از here is the preposition 'concerning / about', and گفته باستان is a noun phrase 'an ancient report/tale' ('گفته' = report/what is told, باستان = ancient).
segments.s241-g61: پر از (= 'full of') governs a list introduced by از; the nouns joined by و enumerate the contents: چاره (means/expedient), مهر (love/affection), نیرنگ (stratagem/deceit), جنگ (war). The whole phrase functions as a descriptive nominal phrase 'full of ...'.
segments.s241-g62: همان (= 'that very one' / 'that') refers back to the foregoing and is followed by از در ('from the door/house of'); مرد فرهنگ و سنگ is a compact epithet: مرد modified by فرهنگ و سنگ ('a man of culture and of stone' — poetic epithets indicating learned/firm character). The sequence gives origin: 'that came from the house/door of the man ...'.
segments.s241-g63: بگفتم is the speaker's past utterance 'I said'. The following sequence is an imperative addressed to the beloved: بیار ای بت خوب‌چهر = 'bring (it), O fair‑faced idol!' (ای introduces the vocative and خوب‌چهر is a compound adjective 'fair‑faced / beautiful of visage').
segments.s241-g64: بخوان is an imperative 'recite/read' with داستان as its object ('recite the tale'). The coordination و بیفزای مهر is a second imperative joined by و: بیفزای = 'add/increase' and مهر = 'affection/love' (i.e. 'and increase the love/affection').
segments.s241-g33: مرا گفت شمعت چه باید همی is arranged with the indirect object before the verb: مرا گفت = "(it) said to me." شمعت (شمع + ـت) is the subject meaning "your candle." چه باید همی is an interrogative clause (چه = "what") with باید (modal "must / what can...") and the archaic particle همی marking the ongoing/habitual or emphatic present; together it yields "what can (it) do? / what should (it) do?".
segments.s241-g34: شب تیره خوابت بباید همی places the noun phrase شب تیره "dark night" before خوابت "your sleep"; بباید (from باید) here has an older/poetic use meaning something like "will befall / will take / will happen to," and همی again gives an archaic present/ emphatic sense. The whole line reads as a prediction/statement: "a dark night will take your sleep" or "your sleep will be in a dark night."
segments.s241-g35: بدو گفتم = "I said to it" (بدو = "to it/unto him" placed before the verb; گفتم = 1st person past "I said"). ای بت is a vocative addressing the beloved as an "idol/beauty." نیم مرد خواب is a predicative phrase meaning "not a man of sleep" (the poet denies being of a sleepy disposition); نیم here is an archaic/poetic negative or negating modifier attached to مرد (this use is idiomatic in classical Persian).
segments.s241-g36: یکی شمع پیش آر چون آفتاب is an imperative request: یکی شمع = "one candle," پیش + آر = "bring forward/before (me)" where پیش is the prepositional element 'before/in front of' and آر is an archaic/poetic imperative meaning 'bring' (here used with پیش). چون آفتاب = "like the sun" is a simile modifying the candle (i.e. make it shine like the sun).
segments.s241-g37: بنه پیشم is an imperative clause telling someone to bring/place a tray/portion before the speaker (بنه is a noun used here as an item to be brought). The conjunction و links that command to a second imperative, بزم را ساز کن, where را marks بزم as a definite direct object and ساز کن is an imperative meaning 'make/prepare (the music/setting)'. Word order is typical classical Persian: imperative/verb with its object following and را marking that object.
segments.s241-g38: به چنگ آر چنگ is an emphatic imperative phrase: به + noun indicates bringing/taking 'into/into the hand' and آر here is an archaic imperative of 'bring/take' (classical usage). The repeated چنگ repeats the object for emphasis. Then و می‌آغاز کن is a periphrastic imperative: present-stem می‌آغاز + auxiliary imperative کن → 'begin!'.
segments.s241-g39: برفت is an older/polished past-tense verb meaning 'he/she left/went away' (classical past form with ب- prefix). آن بت مهربانم is the noun phrase 'that/my kind beloved' (آن = demonstrative, بت used poetically for a beautiful person, مهربانم = 'my kind one' with the bound suffix -م). ز باغ is a prepositional phrase 'from the garden' placed after the verb.
segments.s241-g40: بیاورد here functions as a past narrative verb 'brought' (third-person singular) following the previous verb; it governs the noun phrase رخشنده شمع و چراغ where the adjective رخشنده 'radiant' precedes the coordinated direct objects 'candle and lamp'. The conjunction و simply links the two objects.
segments.s241-g1: شبی چنان‌که (چون) introduces a temporal/comparative clause: 'One night, as/when...'. شبه here is a noun meaning 'likeness, resemblance' modifying روی 'face' (شبهِ روی = 'a face-like likeness' → 'the face as if'); شسته is a past participle 'washed' describing روی, and به قیر uses به + noun to indicate the material/agent ('as if washed in/with pitch'). The clause omits an explicit verb for the moon; it is descriptive background for the following clause.
segments.s241-g2: نه ... نه is the classic Persian correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. پیدا here is adjectival/participial meaning 'visible, apparent' and functions as the predicate ('was visible'); the three names are the items being negated. Word order drops the copula (بود) typical of Persian poetry: 'neither Bahram was visible, nor Kayvān, nor Tir.'
segments.s241-g3: دگرگونه (literally 'otherwise/different') modifies آرایشی ('an adornment/appearance'); آرایشی کرده is a causative/periphrastic expression meaning 'made (an) adornment' or 'assumed an appearance'. ماه is the subject placed after the verb phrase for poetic inversion: 'the moon made a different adornment (i.e. changed its appearance)'.
segments.s241-g4: بسیچ is a quantifier ('many'/'much') modifying گذر; گذر کردن is a light-verb construction meaning 'to pass' (here 'to make a passage' or 'to pass by'). بر پیشگاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/upon the presence (of the throne/court)'. The verb کرده matches گذر (i.e. 'it passed/made many passings before the court').
segments.s241-g5: شده تیره is a perfect/passive-style predicate meaning 'has become dark'. اندر = archaic/poetic 'in, within'. سرای درنگ is a compound: سرای 'abode/house' and درنگ 'delay/pause' → 'the house/place of tarrying' (i.e. the room/space where one waits). The clause states location + resultant state: 'it became dark within the dwelling of delay.'
segments.s241-g6: This clause uses the causative/resultative pattern: میان کرده باریک literally 'made the middle/space narrow' (میان = 'middle/space'); here کرده is the causative/past participle 'made' placed before the adjective باریک for poetic word order. و links the parallel result دل کرده تنگ 'and made the heart tight/constricted' where دل is the object marked only by word order and کرده تنگ is the resultative phrase. In short: 'it narrowed the space and tightened the heart.'
segments.s241-g7: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. تاجش ('his crown') is the genitival phrase. سه بهره 'three shares/portions' is the subject or affected part; شده is the perfect/passive 'became/turned'; لاژورد is the resultant noun (lapis lazuli). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'three portions of his crown became lapis.'
segments.s241-g8: سپرده is a past participle 'entrusted/left' functioning as a verb ('had left/entrusted'); هوا را uses را as the definite object marker (هوا = 'air/atmosphere' here) and به + nouns (به زنگار و گرد) indicates what it was entrusted to/turned over to ('to tarnish and dust'). The clause therefore means '(he/it) had left the air to tarnish and become dusty.'
segments.s241-g25: This is a paired negative: 'نه ... و نه ...' means 'neither ... nor ...'. 'آوای مرغ' (literally 'the voice/sound of the bird') is a genitive phrase with the possessor after the possessed (آوا + یِ + مرغ). 'هرای دد' is another genitive: هرا(ی) = alarm/cry and دد = enemy/hostile one. The whole segment lists two sources of sound that are absent.
segments.s241-g26: زمانه is the subject meaning 'the age/world'. زبان‌بسته is a compound adjective 'tongue‑shut' = 'silent'. از here functions like the English 'about' or 'concerning' (از + noun phrase), introducing the domain 'نیک و بد' = 'good and bad'. Word order is Subject + predicate + complement.
segments.s241-g27: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative past ('نبود' contracted) meaning 'there was not' or 'was not seen'. 'هیچ پیدا' = 'any visible' (هیچ modifying پیدا), 'نشیب از فراز' = 'a slope/descent from the heights' — the phrase places the thing (نشیب) after its modifiers in a compact, poetic order.
segments.s241-g28: دلم is دل + enclitic م = 'my heart'. تنگ شد is a simple past construction: adjective تنگ + auxiliary شد = 'became constricted/grew heavy/was grieved'. زان = 'from that' (archaic/poetic), followed by the temporal phrase 'شب دیریاز' = 'the night long-ago' or 'that long night', giving the cause/time of the grieving.
segments.s241-g29: بدان here is a contracted, archaic locution equivalent to 'به آن/از آن' meaning 'because of that' or 'at that'. تنگی = 'narrowness/distress'. اندر بجستم is poetic: بجستم is an old/personal past-form meaning roughly 'I withdrew/retired/sate myself away' (first‑person past), and ز جای = 'from the place'. The whole clause expresses that the speaker removed himself from his position because of distress.
segments.s241-g30: یکی مهربان is a nominal phrase 'a certain/kind one' (یکی = 'a certain'), followed by بودم (past of بودن). In context بودم functions as the past copula 'was' (the clause means 'there was a certain kind person'). اندر سرای is the locative 'in the house'. Note that older Persian sometimes uses first‑person form impersonally in narration.
segments.s241-g31: خروشیدم is first‑person past 'I cried out/roared' or 'I made an outcry'. و connects clauses. خواستم is 'I wished/asked (for)'. The pair 'ز + و' forms the archaic contraction 'زو' (= از او = 'from her/him'), so 'خواستم زو چراغ' = 'I asked of her a lamp'. In other words, object 'چراغ' is what was requested.
segments.s241-g32: بیامد is simple past 'came'. بت here is used poetically for 'beloved/beauty' (an idol-image for the beloved). مهربانم is 'my kind one' (مهربان + enclitic م = 'my ...'), so بت مهربانم = 'my kind beloved'. به باغ is the direction 'into/to the garden'.
segments.s241-g41: می‌آورد is the imperfective/habitual form of آوردن (here narratively rendered as 'he brought' or 'he would bring'); the conjunction و links a series of direct objects: نار (fire), ترنج (bitter orange/citron), and بهی (quince, here a fruit/ perfume item). The word order lists the items in a simple coordinate series after the verb.
segments.s241-g42: زدوده is a past participle meaning 'cleansed' or 'purified' that modifies or comments on the following noun phrase; یکی جام شاهنشهی is a noun phrase 'one cup (جام) of imperial/royal (شاهنشهی)'. Together the phrase reads as 'a single royal cup, cleansed/purified' (the participle precedes the noun phrase in this poetic word order).
segments.s241-g43: مرا گفت is 'he said to me' (مرا = me + را as object marker; گفت = said) with the subject omitted as is common in Persian. برخیز is an imperative 'rise' or 'get up'; و links the command to the next clause. دل شاددار is an exhortation where شاددار functions as a causative/imperative phrase 'keep/make (your) heart joyful' (poetic compression combines adjective/verb sense).
segments.s241-g44: روان را uses روان 'soul/spirit' as the definite object (را). ز introduces the source 'from' (poetic preposition). درد و غم are the pains and sorrows; آزاد دار is an imperative construction 'keep free / make (it) free' where آزاد is 'free' and دار is the verb element 'hold/keep' in an exhortative sense (classic Persian often places the adjective before دار to form 'make X').
segments.s241-g45: نگر is an imperative 'consider' or 'look' (a poetic command). تا که introduces purpose/result 'so that' and is followed by the clause دل را نداری تباه: literal order places object (دل را) before the verb phrase; نداری here is used in the negative sense 'do not have/let be' or 'you should not let' and تباه is the adjective 'ruined/ruinous' — together meaning 'so that the heart be not ruined' (poetic compactness obscures an explicit auxiliary).
segments.s241-g46: ز introduces source 'from' (poetic). اندیشه و داد are two abstract means 'thought (reason) and justice'; فریاد is 'cry/outcry' and خواه is the imperative of خواستن 'seek/ask' (here used like 'seek a cry' = 'call out for help' or 'appeal'). The clause thus advises: 'seek help from thought and justice.'
segments.s241-g47: جهان 'the world' is the topic. چون گذاری is a poetic clause 'as you pass' or 'when you pass' (چون = as; گذاری from گذشتن/گذاریدن indicates passing), همی is an archaic/prosodic auxiliary meaning roughly 'indeed / will' and بگذرد is the verb 'will pass'. The line states a general truth in parallel: 'the world too will pass as you pass.'
segments.s241-g48: خردمند مردم literally 'the wise people' (adjective before noun, as common in Persian poetry). چرا introduces the question 'why' and غم خورد is a fixed phrase where خورد (here 'befall'/'happen to') combines with غم 'sorrow' to mean 'to suffer grief' or 'to be troubled by sorrow'. The line asks why the sensible among people should be sorrowful when the world passes.
segments.s241-g65: This prepositional phrase opens the line: ز here means 'because of/from' and introduces the cause. نیک و بد ('good and bad') are placed before چرخ ('wheel') in a poetic inversion; چرخ refers to the wheel of fate (the cosmic/fateful wheel). ناسازگار is an adjective modifying چرخ and means 'discordant' or 'uncooperative.'
segments.s241-g66: که begins a relative clause modifying چرخ. آرد is the classical 3rd-person singular present/present-habitual 'brings' (used narratively). به مردم marks the experiencers/recipients ('to/for the people') and ز هرگونه کار ('from every kind of deed') gives the source or variety of outcomes the wheel dispenses.
segments.s241-g67: نگر is an imperative 'look/consider.' تا introduces a purposive or until-clause ('until'). نداری here functions as 'you do not have / you lack' (2nd-person sense), so the clause says 'until you no longer have a narrow heart.' دل خویش تنگ is the noun phrase 'your own heart (دل) + disposition/possessive (خوی + clitic ش) + adjective تنگ (narrow).
segments.s241-g68: بتابی is a present-form meaning 'you are distressed/you fret' (classical usage of تابیدن for emotional disturbance/endurance); ازو is the object 'at/from him/it.' چند here functions interrogatively as 'how long/for how much' and jویی (جویی) is 'you seek/pursue' with درنگ 'delay/hesitation' as its object—together a rhetorical question: 'how long will you seek delay?'
segments.s241-g69: نداند is the classical verb 'knew/did not know' (verb before subject for emphasis). کسی here functions as 'no one' in this negative context. راه و سامان اوی is the object phrase 'his path and his state/condition,' with اوی as the possessive referring back to the person being described.
segments.s241-g70: نه پیدا بود is a negative copular construction: نه (not) + پیدا (apparent) + بود (was) = 'was not apparent.' درد و درمان اوی ('his pain and his cure') are the things that were not visible; اوی again marks possession.
segments.s241-g71: پس آنگه بگفت is a narrative sequence: 'then he said.' ار ز من بشنوی is a conditional clause with ار = 'if' and بشنوی as 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you hear/listen to,' literally 'if you listen to me.'
segments.s241-g72: به شعر marks the medium 'in/into verse' (به + noun). آری here is used emphatically ('indeed' / 'you shall indeed [receive/learn]'), and از دفتر پهلوی gives the source 'from a Pahlavi book'—دفتر = 'book/volume' and پهلوی identifies it as Pahlavi (the Middle Persian language). The verb 'receive/learn' is implied by context.
segments.s241-g17: چنان گشت is a fixed/adverb + past-verb order: چنان (so/thus) modifies گشت (became/was made). باغ و لب جویبار is a coordinated noun phrase ('the garden and the brink/bank of the stream'); لب here means 'brink/edge' and it attaches to جویبار to mean 'stream-bank'. The whole segment states that the garden and the stream-bank 'became' in that way.
segments.s241-g18: کجا is a interrogative/adverb 'where' used rhetorically. موج خیزد uses خیزد (present/old poetic form 'rise') in the sense 'to rise' (here third person singular); with کجا it asks 'where would a wave rise'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', دریای is the genitive form 'sea of ...', and قار is a descriptive noun (here used poetically). The clause is a rhetorical question implying that no wave would rise from that named sea.
segments.s241-g19: فرو ماند is a compound of فرو ('down') + ماند ('remained/was left'), giving a downward/stative result: 'sank down and stayed'. گردون گردان is a poetic apposition: گردون 'the heavens/sky' described as گردان 'turning/revolving'. به جای ('in place') follows to show the heavens stopped 'in their place'.
segments.s241-g20: شده سست is a perfect/passive-like participial phrase 'have become weak'. خورشید را with را topicalizes or marks the sun as affected (object/topic): 'the sun's ...'. دست و پای is a compound 'hands and feet' (metonymically 'strength/power' in Persian poetry). The segment therefore says 'the sun's hands and feet have become weak.'
segments.s241-g21: سپهر is a poetic word for 'the sky/heavens' and اندر آن چادر قیرگون is a prepositional phrase 'within that pitch-black tent' (اندر = 'in/within', آن = 'that', چادر قیرگون = 'pitch-black tent'). The phrase places the sky metaphorically inside a dark tent.
segments.s241-g22: تُو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. گفتی and شدستی here appear in an archaic/poetic verbal sequence meaning roughly 'was said' / 'seemed to have become' (literally 'you said/you became'); the lexical senses provided do not list the verbal meanings, so those are marked below with -1. به خواب اندرون is the prepositional phrase 'into sleep inside' (به = 'into/towards', خواب = 'sleep', اندرون = 'within'). Grammatically this segment reports that the addressee was thought/said to be asleep inside the dark tent.
segments.s241-g23: جهان را دل is a topical inversion: جهان را (with را marking topic) + دل 'the heart (of the world)'. از خویشتن پر هراس breaks down as از خویشتن 'from/of itself' + پر 'full (of)' + هراس 'fear/terror' — 'the world's heart, full of terror at itself.'
segments.s241-g24: جرس برکشیده is a perfect/participle phrase 'having raised the bell' or 'raised the bell' (برکشیده = 'lifted/raised'). نگهبان پاس are apposed nouns meaning 'the guard, the sentry/watch' (نگهبان = 'guard', پاس = 'sentry/watch'); the sequence describes a sentry who has raised or sounded the bell.
segments.s241-g49: گهی ... و گه is a correlative pair meaning 'sometimes ... and sometimes ...' (classical/poetic usage). می‌گسارید is a present/habitual 2nd-person (plural or polite) form with the progressive/present prefix می- plus the verb گساریدن, here 'scatter' or 'let loose'. ساخت is a past tense verb (ساخت) meaning 'made/played' placed after its object چنگ (harp); in classical Persian ساخت can mean 'played (an instrument)' or 'made', and the sentence mixes a present habitual clause with a past simple clause for poetic effect.
segments.s241-g50: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person singular past 'you said' (گفتی); که is the complementizer 'that', introducing the content clause هاروت نیرنگ ساخت where هاروت is the grammatical subject and نیرنگ ساخت is a past‑tense clause 'devised/made a trick'. In poetry the verbal forms and subjects may be compacted, but the syntax is standard: '[You] said that Harut contrived a deception.'
segments.s241-g51: دلم is دل + enclitic ـم 'my heart' (possessive suffix). پیروز کردن with بر + noun expresses 'to be victorious over' or 'to triumph over' (پیروز + بر + complement); here بر همه کام = 'over every desire'. کرد is the past-tense light verb forming the predicate 'triumphed' (i.e. 'my heart triumphed over all desires').
segments.s241-g52: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. بر من here means 'upon me' / 'for me'. شب تیره is a noun + adjective ('dark night'), with the adjective following the noun in classical order. نوروز کرد is a verbal phrase where نوروز (Nowruz, 'new day'/'new year') is the object and کرد 'made' means 'caused to be' or 'turned into', so literally 'the dark night made/turned (for) me a Nowruz' (a metaphorical renewal or new dawn).
segments.s241-g53: بدان is the classical contraction of به آن 'to that' (preposition + demonstrative). سرو بن here addresses the cypress (سرو) with بن 'base/root' used poetically (often to mean 'tall/cypress' or to specify the tree), and گفتم is 1st-person past 'I said'. ای ماهروی is a vocative phrase 'O moon-faced one', with ای marking direct address and ماهروی an adjective-noun ('moon-faced', i.e. beautiful).
segments.s241-g54: یکی is an indefinite/number word 'one' or 'a certain' used adverbially here ('one tale'). داستان is the noun 'story/tale'. امشبم is امشب 'tonight' with the possessive enclitic ـم 'my/this night of mine' (classical poetic usage). بازگوی is an imperative or jussive form from بازگفتن / بازگو 'recount, tell' placed at the end (typical SOV order in Persian poetry): 'tell (me)'.
segments.s241-g55: که introduces the content: دل گیرد is a present/formal clause where گیرد (گرفتن) means 'takes/seizes' or 'is seized' — classical Persian often allows this ambiguous stative reading 'the heart is seized'. از مهر او means 'by his love' or 'with love for him'. فر و مهر pairs two nouns 'far (glory)' and 'mehr (affection)', coordinated by و; this parallelism lists qualities associated with the beloved.
segments.s241-g56: بدو is به + او 'to him' / 'unto him' (or 'for him') and functions as a prepositional/dative phrase. اندرون 'within' is an adverbial/locative. خیره ماند is a compound meaning 'remained astonished/staring' (خیره as predicate/adverb and ماند 'remained' as the past tense of ماندن). سپهر 'the heavens/sky' is placed after the verb for poetic inversion, so the literal order yields 'the heavens remained astonished within him.'
segments.s241-g9: This is a noun phrase + prepositional phrase: سپاه (host/army) is modified postpositively by شب تیره (literally “night dark/ominous”), a poetic apposition rather than the colloquial ezafe construction. بر + noun (بر دشت و راغ) is a locative/prepositional phrase meaning “over/on the plain and راغ.” Word order (modifier after noun, preposition before place) is typical of Persian poetry and omits connecting particles for meter.
segments.s241-g10: A verbless nominal simile: یکی here functions as an indefinite/indefinite-simile marker (“as if / as one”), فرش گسترده is a noun (carpet) + adjective/participle (spread out), and از introduces the material/composition (from/of). The participle گسترده (= ‘spread out’) stands after the noun in poetic word order; از + پر زاغ indicates the carpet is made of crow/raven feathers.
segments.s241-g11: نمودم is an intransitive past used impersonally: nمود (appear/show) plus enclitic -م giving a dative reading “appeared to me / was shown to me.” ز هر سو is a circumstantial phrase “from every side/direction.” به چشم here marks the experiencer/domain (“to the eye / to the sight”), and اهرمن is the noun in the phrase (the demon/Ahriman). The clause therefore means something like “it appeared from every side to (my) sight as Ahriman.”
segments.s241-g12: A simile introduced by چو (“like/as”): مار سیه = “black snake/serpent” (noun + adjective), and باز کرده دهن is a participial clause where کرده is the perfect participle ‘having done/caused’ modifying دهن. Poetic inversion places باز کرده before دهن (standard prose would be دهن باز کرده). The whole phrase reads “like a black serpent that had opened its mouth.”
segments.s241-g13: چو introduces a simile “as/like.” پولاد (steel) is the comparison term; زنگار خورده is a compound participial phrase meaning “rust-eaten / tarnished” (زنگار = rust, خورده = eaten/worn), and سپهر (sky/heaven) is the noun being likened. Word order places the participial modifier before the head noun in poetic style: “the sky, as if rust-eaten steel.”
segments.s241-g14: تو گفتی is an address + speech verb in past tense “you said.” به قیر اندر اندود is a locative/agentless passive-like description where به قیر = “with/in tar (pitch),” اندر (in/within) and اندود (a coating/smear, here used as participial/adjectival) modify چهر (face/appearance). The literal reading: “you said (that) the face was coated within with tar.”
segments.s241-g15: هرآنگه که is a temporal/subordinating phrase meaning “whenever/whensoever.” برزد is an archaic/poetic past verb meaning “struck / came / blew” (here used of wind), and یکی باد سرد = “a cold wind” (یکی = an indefinite numeral/particle, باد = wind, سرد = cold). The clause reads “whenever a cold wind blew/struck.”
segments.s241-g16: چو introduces a comparison/simile again. برانگیخت is a past verb “raised/roused.” ز marks origin/source (“from”), انگشت = “finger,” and گرد here is the noun “dust” (poetic word order places گرد after انگشت). The image: “it raised from the finger a circle/whirl of dust,” with زنگی (here ambiguous/poetic) acting as a comparative image (see senses note).

sentences.s241-s37: I declare my resolve, and I also accept thanks; now listen, O pair who recognize goodness.
sentences.s241-s29: Hear me, my kind beloved — what he said after we, with our desire fulfilled, were joined as a pair.
sentences.s241-s30: Stay/hold on until one tale; I will tell you of an ancient saying.
sentences.s241-s31: Full of devices and love and deceit and war — that (very) one came from the house/door of the man of culture and stone.
sentences.s241-s32: I said, 'Bring (it), O fair‑faced idol; recite the tale and increase affection.'
sentences.s241-s17: It said to me, "What can your candle do? In the dark night your sleep will be taken."
sentences.s241-s18: I said to it, "O idol, I am not a man given to sleep; bring one candle forward, like the sun."
sentences.s241-s19: Set a tray before me and make ready the feast; take the harp in hand and begin to play.
sentences.s241-s20: My kind beloved left the garden and brought a radiant candle and lamp.
sentences.s241-s1: One night, when the moon's face looked as if it had been washed with pitch, neither Bahram (Mars) nor Kayvān (Saturn) nor Tir could be seen.
sentences.s241-s2: The moon assumed a different appearance; it made many a passage before the court.
sentences.s241-s3: It grew dark inside the house of delay; it made the space narrow and the heart constricted.
sentences.s241-s4: From his crown three shares had turned to lapis‑lazuli; he had left the air to tarnish and to dust.
sentences.s241-s13: Neither the song of the bird nor the alarm of the enemy — the age is mute about good and evil.
sentences.s241-s14: No descent could be seen from the heights; my heart grew tight because of that long-ago night.
sentences.s241-s15: Because of that distress I withdrew from my place; there was a certain kind one in the house.
sentences.s241-s16: I cried out and begged of her a lamp; my kind beloved came into the garden.
sentences.s241-s21: He brought fire and citron and fragrant quince, and a single cup of imperial wine, purified.
sentences.s241-s22: He said to me, 'Rise, and make your heart glad; free the soul from pain and sorrow.'
sentences.s241-s23: Consider this so that the heart not be ruined; from thought and justice seek a cry (ask for help).
sentences.s241-s24: The world, as you pass through it, also will pass; why should the wise among people grieve?
sentences.s241-s33: Because of the discordant wheel of good and bad, which brings to people every sort of outcome.
sentences.s241-s34: Consider (reflect) until you no longer have a narrow heart; why be vexed at him, for how long will you seek delay?
sentences.s241-s35: No one knew his way and condition; his pain and his remedy were not apparent.
sentences.s241-s36: Then he said, 'If you listen to me, you will indeed receive it in verse from a Pahlavi book.'
sentences.s241-s9: Thus the garden and the bank of the stream became so — where would a wave rise from the sea of Qar?
sentences.s241-s10: The revolving heavens sank down and remained in place; the sun's hands and feet became weak.
sentences.s241-s11: The sky lay within that pitch-black tent; you were said/seen to have fallen into sleep inside.
sentences.s241-s12: The heart of the world, filled with fear of itself; a sentry has lifted the bell.
sentences.s241-s25: Sometimes you scattered, and sometimes you played the harp; you said that Harut devised a deception.
sentences.s241-s26: My heart triumphed over every desire, for the dark night made (for) me a Nowruz.
sentences.s241-s27: To that cypress I said, 'O moon-faced one, tell me one tale of this night.'
sentences.s241-s28: That the heart is seized by his love; his glory and affection — within, the heavens remained astonished.
sentences.s241-s5: The dark host of night lay over plain and meadow — as if a carpet of crow-feathers had been spread.
sentences.s241-s6: From every side appeared, to (my) sight, Ahriman — like a black serpent that had opened its mouth.
sentences.s241-s7: The sky was like steel eaten by rust; you said its face was smeared within with pitch.
sentences.s241-s8: Whenever a cold wind blew, it raised — as if a zangi — a ring/whorl of dust from the finger.
